findings.description: Docstring promises strict YYYY-MM-DD parsing, but strptime accepts non-zero-padded month/day (e.g. '2024-1-5') because leading zeros are optional for %m and %d.
datetime.strptime is roughly an order of magnitude slower than date.fromisoformat for this fixed format, which only matters in hot parsing paths.
Module docstring claims 'pure functions' but utc_now() and humanize_delta's default reference read the system clock, which is impure.
Day-boundary helpers hardcode UTC with no timezone parameter, limiting reuse for local-time day windows (documented, so likely intentional).
datetime instances satisfy the date type hint (subclass) and are silently accepted; subtraction floors partial days and the returned items are datetimes with time components, not pure dates.
day_range materializes the entire list; a generator would avoid O(n) memory for very large spans (millions of date objects across wide year ranges).
is_weekend is the only public function in the module without a docstring.
weekday() >= 5 hardcodes a Saturday/Sunday weekend, which is wrong for locales with Friday/Saturday weekends if the helper is used internationally.
The name business_days_between suggests holiday awareness but only weekends are excluded; the docstring is accurate yet the name may mislead callers.
Business-day counting is O(n) over a fully materialized day list; a closed-form weekday arithmetic formula computes the same result in O(1).

The PEP 604 'datetime | None' annotation is evaluated at definition time (no __future__ annotations import), so the module fails to import on Python < 3.10; only an issue if older interpreters must be supported.
The awareness check only tests tzinfo is None; a tzinfo whose utcoffset() returns None still makes the datetime naive, so the subtraction raises TypeError instead of the documented ValueError.
int() truncates toward zero, so moments up to one second in the future report 'just now' rather than 'in the future'.
No month or year tiers exist, so multi-year deltas render as large week counts (e.g. '104 w ago'), which is weak humanization even for a self-described coarse helper.
clamp_to_range performs no tz-awareness validation, so mixing naive and aware datetimes raises a bare TypeError, inconsistent with humanize_delta's explicit ValueError contract.
